meta.title: Functional analysis of secA homologues from rickettsiae

abstractHtml: The molecular basis of protein secretion that underlines rickettsial pathogenesis remains unknown. This paper reports the molecular and functional analysis of the putative secA gene, an essential component of the Sec-dependent protein secretion pathway, from Rickettsia rickettsii and Rickettsia typhi, the aetiological agents of Rocky Mountain spotted fever and murine typhus, respectively. The sequence analysis of the cloned secA genes from R. rickettsii and R. typhi show ORFs of 2721 and 2718 nt, respectively. Alignment of the deduced amino acid sequences reveals the presence of highly conserved amino acid residues and motifs considered to be essential for the ATPase activity of SecA in preprotein translocation. Transcription analysis indicates that R. rickettsii secA is expressed monocistronically from the canonical prokaryotic promoter, with a transcriptional start point located 32 nt upstream of the secA initiation codon. Complementation analysis shows that the full-length SecA protein from R. rickettsii and R. typhi fails to restore growth of the temperature-sensitive Escherichia coli strain MM52 secA51(ts) at a non-permissive temperature (42 °C), despite the detection of SecA protein expression by Western blotting. However, the chimeric SecA protein carrying the N-terminal 408 aa of R. rickettsii SecA fused with the C-terminal 480 aa of E. coli SecA restores the growth of E. coli strain MM52 secA51(ts) at the non-permissive temperature (42 °C). These results suggest that the N-terminal ATPase domain is highly conserved, whereas the C-terminal domain appears to be species specific.
